choiceresponse.label: Check the Lime explainer output for the fifth sentence: “You qualify for a $25 voucher based on recent activity—reply YES to receive details. Reply STOP to opt out.” Which conclusions are supported by the LIME explanation? (Select all that apply.)

checkboxgroup.choice: Words like “voucher”, “reply YES”, and “STOP” strongly increase spam probability.
The model treats opt-out language as evidence of legitimacy.
The prediction is driven primarily by call-to-action language, not by the numerical amount of the offer.
LIME assigns little importance to background phrasing that does not request an action from the user.
The explanation shows that most words in the sentence contribute equally to the spam prediction.

div.p: The LIME explanation concentrates weight on a small set of action-oriented tokens, such as “voucher”, “reply YES”, and “STOP”, which are strongly associated with promotional spam. The dollar amount itself is not highlighted, indicating that the model responds more to response instructions than to the size of the incentive. In contrast, contextual phrases like “based on recent activity” receive little or no weight, showing that not all words contribute equally to the prediction. This uneven distribution of importance directly contradicts the claim that all tokens matter to a similar degree.
